summary: Fowler addresses whether reordering declarations (like methods and fields) in code constitutes refactoring. He acknowledges the counterargument that since declaration order doesn't affect program execution, it shouldn't count as refactoring. However, he argues that since his definition of refactoring includes making code easier to understand and cheaper to modify, and reordering can improve understandability by grouping related features or explaining how a class works, it qualifies as refactoring. He dismisses execution impact as a red herring, noting that renaming methods also doesn't alter execution yet is clearly refactoring.
key_points: Declaration order in modern languages doesn't affect program execution, leading some to argue reordering isn't refactoring
Refactoring's definition includes making code easier to understand and cheaper to modify, not just changing behavior
Grouping related features together and ordering to explain class functionality improves understandability
The 'doesn't alter execution' argument is a red herring since renaming also doesn't change execution but is clearly refactoring
money_quotes: The order in which you declare features in modern programming languages doesn't alter the program at all.
In my DefinitionOfRefactoring, I used the phrase 'to make it easier to understand and cheaper to modify'.
It can make sense to put related features in a class together.
You can also order the features in such a way to help explain how the class works.
The fact that it doesn't alter the internal execution is a red-herring.
Changing the name of a method doesn't alter the execution, yet renaming is a very important refactoring to improve the understandability of a program.
key_insight: Refactoring is defined by improving understandability and modifiability, not by whether it changes program execution.
